Description: Iraq invaded Kuwait triggering the Gulf War. Immediate supply disruption fears sent oil prices spiking from ~$17 to $46/barrel within weeks.
DataProvenance: Official US DoD / UN Security Council records; exact date confirmed

Description: Coalition forces launched Operation Desert Storm. Prices initially surged then fell as the war was brief and supply disruptions were contained.
DataProvenance: US DoD; confirmed date 17 Jan 1991

Description: Thai baht devalued triggering the Asian Financial Crisis. Demand for oil collapsed across Southeast Asia pushing Brent from ~$19 to below $10 by end 1998.
DataProvenance: Crisis onset conventionally dated 2 Jul 1997 (baht float). Some analysts use the broader Jul–Aug 1997 window. Attribution as primary cause is contested – coincident USD strengthening also significant.

Description: OPEC and non-OPEC producers agreed to cut output by 2.1 million bpd at the Vienna meeting. This stabilised the market and began a multi-year recovery above $20/barrel.
DataProvenance: OPEC official communiqué 23 Mar 1999

Description: Al-Qaeda attacks on the US created deep uncertainty. Oil briefly spiked then fell as recession fears dominated demand outlook. Prices remained subdued through 2002.
DataProvenance: Confirmed historical record

Description: Coalition forces invaded Iraq. Supply fears had already lifted prices during the military build-up. Post-invasion prices remained elevated due to insurgency disrupting Iraqi output.
DataProvenance: US DoD; confirmed 20 Mar 2003

Description: China's rapid industrialisation drove unprecedented global oil demand growth from 2004 onward, underpinning the commodity supercycle and sustained multi-year price rise.
DataProvenance: Date is approximate – Jan 2004 is a conventional annual boundary used by IEA to mark the breakout year for Chinese demand. The actual inflection in the data spans 2003–2005. Attribution to China alone is contested; India and Middle Eastern demand also contributed.

Description: Category 5 hurricane made landfall on the US Gulf Coast, shutting in ~1.5 million bpd and causing refinery damage. US strategic reserves were released for the first time since Gulf War.
DataProvenance: NOAA / US National Hurricane Center; confirmed 29 Aug 2005

EventName: US Subprime Mortgage Market Stress Begins
Description: Delinquencies in US subprime mortgages began rising sharply. Though the full market crisis did not erupt until mid-2007, this period marked the beginning of the credit deterioration that would eventually trigger the GFC.
DataProvenance: Date approximate – stress was building throughout 2006–Q1 2007. No single event date; Jan 2007 is a model convenience anchor. Attribution as a discrete oil price driver at this date is contested.
ChangePointProximity: Not directly linked to model CP

Description: Brent crude reached its historical intraday peak of $147.50, driven by peak demand, speculative commodity index flows, a weak USD, and tight OPEC spare capacity.
DataProvenance: ICE Brent spot data; confirmed 11 Jul 2008

Description: Lehman Brothers filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy, triggering the Global Financial Crisis. Brent crashed from $147 to under $40 by December 2008 – the largest single-year price collapse on record.
DataProvenance: US Bankruptcy Court filing; confirmed 15 Sep 2008

Description: OPEC extended production cuts agreed at the December 2008 meeting. Combined with global fiscal stimulus packages, this put a floor under prices and began a multi-year recovery.
DataProvenance: OPEC official communiqué; date confirmed. Recovery attributed jointly to OPEC cuts and G20 stimulus – contested as single cause.

Description: Mohamed Bouazizi's self-immolation in Sidi Bouzid triggered widespread protests and the Arab Spring. The movement spread to Libya in early 2011, directly disrupting ~1.5 million bpd of output.
DataProvenance: Date confirmed. Attribution of oil price impact to this specific date is contested – oil market impact was felt primarily in Feb–Mar 2011 when Libyan disruption became severe. The Dec 2010 date is a political precursor, not a direct supply shock.

Description: Protests in Benghazi escalated into armed conflict, rapidly halting Libyan crude exports (~1.5 million bpd). Brent rose from ~$100 to a $126 peak in April 2011 before recovering.
DataProvenance: UN Security Council records; Libyan export disruption confirmed from mid-Feb 2011

Description: US tight-oil (shale) production reached record levels (~8.5 mbpd), creating a structural global supply surplus that OPEC had not yet countered. This multi-month process culminated in the Nov 2014 OPEC decision.
DataProvenance: Date is approximate – Jun 2014 is when EIA data shows the peak quarter. The shale supply glut built throughout 2013–2014. Attribution of a discrete price break to this exact date is contested.

Description: OPEC, led by Saudi Arabia, decided not to cut production despite a global supply glut and falling prices, effectively abandoning its swing-producer role. Brent collapsed from ~$85 to below $50 over the following months.
DataProvenance: OPEC official communiqué 27 Nov 2014

Description: Iran returned to the oil market after international sanctions were lifted following the JCPOA nuclear deal. Added ~1 million bpd of Iranian crude to a market already in surplus.
DataProvenance: IAEA Implementation Day declared 16 Jan 2016

Description: OPEC and Russia agreed to cut output by 1.8 million bpd in the landmark OPEC+ deal. Prices rose from ~$45 to above $55/barrel within weeks of the announcement.
DataProvenance: OPEC official communiqué 30 Nov 2016

Description: Trump administration reimposed comprehensive sanctions on Iran after withdrawing from the JCPOA, effectively removing ~1–1.5 million bpd from global supply and contributing to prices above $80.
DataProvenance: US Treasury Executive Order; sanctions effective 5 Nov 2018

Description: Drone and cruise missile strikes on Abqaiq and Khurais processing facilities temporarily knocked out ~5.7 million bpd (~5% of global supply). Brent spiked over 15% on 16 Sep before recovering.
DataProvenance: Saudi Aramco / Saudi Aramco incident report; US intelligence assessment confirms attack

EventName: Saudi Arabia–Russia Oil Price War Begins
Description: OPEC+ talks collapsed. Saudi Arabia pledged to flood the market by raising output and slashing OSPs. Coinciding with COVID-19 demand collapse, Brent fell to 20-year lows within weeks.
DataProvenance: Saudi ARAMCO official OSP releases; confirmed 8 Mar 2020

Description: OPEC+ agreed the largest coordinated production cut in history (9.7 million bpd) in an emergency virtual meeting. This put a floor under prices and began a slow recovery as demand returned.
DataProvenance: OPEC official communiqué 12 Apr 2020

Description: WTI May 2020 futures settled at -$37.63/barrel due to storage capacity constraints. Brent did not go negative but fell below $20 – its lowest price since 2002.
DataProvenance: NYMEX settlement data; confirmed 20 Apr 2020. Note: Brent did not go negative – WTI anomaly is linked.

Description: Mass vaccination programmes began globally in Q1 2021, fuelling firm expectations of demand recovery. Brent rose steadily from ~$50 in Jan to $70+ by mid-2021.
DataProvenance: Date is approximate – the UK began on 8 Dec 2020; US and EU scaled up from Jan 2021. Jan 2021 used as model anchor for mass-scale global rollout. Price impact was gradual rather than discrete.

Description: Russia launched a full-scale land invasion of Ukraine triggering sweeping Western sanctions on Russian oil and gas exports. Brent surged above $130 in March 2022 – the highest since the 2008 peak.
DataProvenance: Ukrainian Ministry of Defence; UN General Assembly records; confirmed 24 Feb 2022
